idioms.definition: By informal communication; by rumour or gossip (also: hear sth on the grapevine)
examples: I heard on the grapevine that the company is planning to merge with a rival.
She found out through the grapevine that her colleague was leaving.
News travels fast in a small office – it came through the grapevine before any official announcement.
idioms.usageTip: Most commonly used in the phrase 'hear something on/through the grapevine'. It implies the information is unofficial and may not be entirely reliable. Not suitable for formal communication.

idioms.definition: Not knowing about something; kept uninformed
examples: The employees were kept in the dark about the restructuring plans for months.
I was completely in the dark about what had happened until she told me.
Why was I left in the dark about such an important decision?
idioms.usageTip: Often used with 'keep someone in the dark' or 'be in the dark'. The phrase implies that information has been deliberately or accidentally withheld.

idioms.definition: To regularly update someone with the latest news or information
examples: I'll keep you posted on any developments with the contract.
Please keep me posted – I want to know as soon as there's news.
She asked her doctor to keep her posted on the test results.
idioms.usageTip: A useful phrase in both professional and personal contexts. It signals ongoing communication rather than a one-off update. Often used as a reassurance or a request.

idioms.definition: To keep something secret; not to tell anyone
examples: I'll tell you what happened, but please keep it under your hat for now.
The plans for the surprise party have to be kept under your hat.
Can I trust you to keep this under your hat until the announcement is made?
idioms.usageTip: Used when asking someone to keep information confidential. It has a slightly informal, conversational tone. Often used as a direct request: 'Keep this under your hat.'

idioms.definition: To reach an obvious conclusion from available information
examples: When I saw the empty desk and cleared computer, I put two and two together.
She noticed the ring on his finger and quickly put two and two together.
It didn't take long to put two and two together once I saw the evidence.
idioms.usageTip: Used to describe deducing something logical from clues. Sometimes extended to 'put two and two together and make five', meaning to draw the wrong conclusion from the available facts.

idioms.definition: A person who cannot keep secrets or talks too much
examples: Don't tell him anything – he's got a real big mouth.
She's such a big mouth; she told the whole office about the salary changes.
I knew I was a big mouth the moment I let it slip at the party.
idioms.usageTip: Usually critical or humorous in tone. Can be used as a noun ('a big mouth') or in a phrase like 'have a big mouth' or 'me and my big mouth' (said after accidentally revealing something).

fillInExercises.sentence: I heard ___ that the director is planning to resign before the end of the year.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On the grapevine' means hearing something through informal channels or rumour, not through an official source.

fillInExercises.sentence: The staff felt frustrated because management had kept them ___ about the new policy for weeks.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'In the dark' means not being informed about something, often deliberately. 'Keep someone in the dark' is the full phrase.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'll ___ as soon as I hear anything back from the interview panel.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Keep someone posted' means to give them regular updates about a situation as it develops.

fillInExercises.sentence: This is strictly confidential – please ___ until we make the official announcement.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Keep something under your hat' means to keep it secret and not tell anyone.

fillInExercises.sentence: When she saw the resignation letter on the printer and the boxes in his office, she quickly ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put two and two together' means to draw an obvious conclusion from available clues or evidence.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't share the surprise with Marcus – he's a complete ___ and he'll ruin everything.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A big mouth' refers to someone who cannot keep secrets or who talks too much and reveals confidential information.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Have you heard? Apparently, the whole marketing team is being moved to a different floor. I heard it [on the grapevine|on the grapevine].

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Really? Why was I [in the dark|in the dark] about this? No one tells me anything.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I think management wanted to keep it quiet. I only know because Jake mentioned it – and he's a complete [big mouth|big mouth].

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Did he say anything else? When is it happening?

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: He wasn't sure. I'm waiting to hear from a contact in HR. I'll [keep you posted|keep you posted] when I know more.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Please do. And in the meantime, let's [keep this under our hats|keep sth under your hat] – I don't want to start unnecessary rumours.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: I've been [in the dark|in the dark] about why the project was delayed for weeks. No one told me a thing.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: I know a bit more, but I promised to [keep it under my hat|keep sth under your hat] until the manager is ready to explain.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Fair enough. I suppose I already [put two and two together|put two and two together] when I saw the contract had been amended.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: You're sharp. Did anyone else notice?

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: I heard [on the grapevine|on the grapevine] that a few people in accounts had guessed, but nothing official.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: I'll ask the manager to say something today. I'll [keep you posted|keep you posted] as soon as there's news.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: not knowing about something; kept uninformed
to regularly update someone with the latest news
to keep something secret; not to tell anyone
to reach an obvious conclusion from available information
a person who cannot keep secrets or talks too much

quizExercises.sentence: I found out about the merger ___ – it hadn't been officially confirmed yet.
quizExercises.explanation: 'On the grapevine' describes learning something through informal rumour or gossip rather than an official source.

quizExercises.sentence: As soon as the investigation results are ready, I'll ___ – you won't be waiting long.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Keep someone posted' means to give them regular updates on a developing situation.

quizExercises.question: Which situation best illustrates 'keep it under your hat'?
options: A manager announces a promotion in a team meeting so everyone hears at once
A colleague tells you about a new hire but asks you not to mention it before HR sends the official email
A friend posts exciting news on social media before telling anyone in person
quizExercises.explanation: 'Keep it under your hat' means to keep information secret for the time being, exactly as in option B where the information must not be shared yet.

quizExercises.question: Which scenario best shows someone 'putting two and two together'?
options: A student guesses the answer to a maths problem without any working
An employee notices a colleague's desk has been cleared and a job advert posted online for the same role, and concludes the person has left
A manager reads the end-of-year financial report and shares it with the board
quizExercises.explanation: 'Put two and two together' means to draw a logical conclusion from separate pieces of evidence, as in option B.
